questions.question: Which wavelength has the lowest attenuation in standard single-mode optical fiber?
questions.explanation: 1550 nm (the telecom C-band) has the lowest attenuation at approximately 0.2 dB/km. This makes it ideal for long-distance fiber-based quantum communication.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of time-bin encoding over polarization encoding in fiber?
questions.explanation: Fiber birefringence causes random polarization rotations that require active compensation. Time-bin encoding is inherently robust against these effects, making it preferred for long-distance fiber QKD.

questions.question: What is the typical core diameter of single-mode fiber used for quantum communication?
questions.explanation: Single-mode fiber has a core diameter of approximately 8-10 μm (typically 9 μm). The 125 μm dimension is the cladding diameter. Multimode fibers have 50 or 62.5 μm cores.

questions.question: What is the g²(0) value for an ideal single-photon source?
questions.explanation: g²(0) = 0 indicates perfect antibunching—exactly one photon per pulse. Coherent light (laser) has g²(0) = 1, and thermal light has g²(0) = 2. Values below 0.5 indicate single-photon character.

questions.question: Which single-photon detector technology offers the highest detection efficiency at 1550 nm?
questions.explanation: SNSPDs achieve >90% detection efficiency at telecom wavelengths, far exceeding APDs (~25%) and PMTs (not sensitive at 1550 nm). The trade-off is that SNSPDs require cryogenic cooling to ~2.5 K.

questions.question: What is the primary disadvantage of weak coherent pulse (WCP) sources for QKD?

options.text: Multi-photon pulses enable photon-number splitting attacks

questions.explanation: WCP sources follow Poisson statistics, meaning some pulses contain 2+ photons. An eavesdropper can split off extra photons without disturbing the transmitted one. Decoy-state protocols mitigate this vulnerability.

questions.question: After 50 km of standard single-mode fiber at 1550 nm, approximately 1% of photons survive.
questions.explanation: 50 km × 0.2 dB/km = 10 dB loss. Transmission = 10^(-10/10) = 0.1 = 10%. Actually about 10%, so the statement is approximately correct (1% would be 100 km). The exact value at 50 km is ~10%.
